one_line_takeaway: A four-prompt "persona"/role-prediction jailbreak that incrementally poisons an LLM's conversational memory drives GPT-4o to 95% attack-success (88% fully-detailed) and defeats production ChatGPT, Claude 3.7, and Grok where classic single-prompt attacks (GCG, Jailbroken) score 0%.
key_finding: On GPT-4o, the Sequential-1 arrangement reaches 95.0% ASR and 88.3% FAR, versus 75.0%/63.3% for the single-prompt "Once" variant, while prior baselines Jailbroken and Parameters (Huang et al. catastrophic generation-exploit) both score 0%/0% ASR/FAR in the same setting and GCG could not even be optimized on black-box GPT-4o (Figure 4 reports the baselines at 0 success while Persona is at least 63.3%). Memory implementation matters: on GPT-4o, Sequential-1 ASR is 95 under state-based memory vs 88.33 under manual memory; on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct the gap is larger (Sequential-1 ASR 35 state-based vs 5 manual; Once is 20 for both). Notably, Llama shows a counterintuitive reversal — Once (20% ASR) beats Sequential (5% manual), which the authors attribute to Llama's many-shot-jailbreak (MSJ) safety training recognizing the multi-turn accumulation pattern. Appendix combination table (gpt-4o-2024-11-20) reports Sequential-1, Sequential-2, and Additional-4 all at 95.0% ASR (Additional-4 and Sequential-2 at 90.0% FAR). Generalizing to all 520 AdvBench prompts, 440/520 were judged successful for a FAR of 84.61%. Theme-level, Fake-Information/Fraud and Unlicensed Activity hit 100% ASR and FAR. Real-world attacks succeeded against ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and Grok 3 Beta (with shared transcript URLs); the authors state Claude is "relatively robust but still has a critical FAR."
core_contribution: The paper proposes "Persona Attack," a multi-turn / memory-injection jailbreak against LLMs. Instead of a single adversarial prompt, the attacker sends four prompts: three "instruction imprinting" prompts (I1 Imitation — ask the model to predict how other LLMs would respond to a query, I2 Assumption Injection — frame the query as a format-following task with four fixed response cases from "Jailbreak Fail" to "Jailbreak Success," I3 Unmasking — demand a complete, unmasked answer) followed by the harmful question Qh wrapped in the query format. The authors frame prompt ordering as a combinatorial optimization problem (max ASR over combinations of I1,I2,I3,Qh via strategies sequential-1, sequential-2, once, additional), pruned to high-ASR configurations. They also introduce a distinction between two memory realizations — "manual memory" (prior turns concatenated and pasted before each new prompt) and "state-based memory" (an internal state updated by a memory function each turn) — and argue the implementation itself materially affects attack success. The claimed root cause is a weighting imbalance P = alpha*P_instruction + beta*P_safety with alpha > beta, i.e. accumulated user instructions in memory come to dominate safety alignment.
threat_model: A black-box, interactive adversary who can hold a multi-turn conversation with a deployed chat LLM (no weights, gradients, or internal access required — GCG-style optimization is explicitly infeasible here). The attacker sends benign-looking "instruction imprinting" turns that reframe the model as predicting another LLM's output and classifying responses into fixed "jailbreak success/fail" cases, so that accumulated instructions in the model's memory/context come to outweigh its safety alignment; the final harmful request is then answered as a "legitimate simulation." Exploited surface is the model's memory/context mechanism; the attack requires only the ability to continuously influence conversation history, and the authors note its efficacy is tied to how accessible/updatable that memory is.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: because target models (GPT-4o) are black boxes, they can only observe the bypass at the output level and cannot directly inspect how the internal alignment/safety mechanism is disturbed, weakening interpretation and generalization; the opacity also hampers designing defenses; they defer actual mitigation (e.g., detecting/purging malicious instructions from memory, periodic long-context reset) to future work. Reviewer-noted grounding caveats: the real-world commercial results (Figure 5) are reported only qualitatively in the text (no numeric ASR/FAR table extracted for Claude/Grok); the 520-prompt generalization ASR/FAR is scored by automated refusal-phrase matching, which can overcount; the paper is a rough preprint with internally inconsistent citation numbering (e.g., GCG cited variously as [9]/[10], [14], and [15]) and some placeholder-style phrasing ("we plan to conduct experiments"). Small open model (Llama-3.2-3B) and only two backbone models limit scope.

datasets_used: AdvBench (60 harmful prompts across 6 themes, built on 40 harmful questions from Liu et al. [29])
quantitative_results: Baselines Jailbroken and Parameters: 0% ASR / 0% FAR on GPT-4o; GCG not optimizable on black-box GPT-4o (Fig 4 / Table 1)
GPT-4o Sequential-1 memory: 95 ASR state-based vs 88.33 ASR manual; FAR 88.33 vs 83.33 (Table 2)
Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct Sequential-1: 35% ASR state-based vs 5% manual; Once 20% both (Table 2)
Appendix combos (gpt-4o-2024-11-20): Sequential-1 95.0/88.3, Sequential-2 95.0/90.0, Additional-4 95.0/90.0, Once 73.3/63.3 (Table 4)
Theme-level 100% ASR & FAR for Fake-Information/Fraud and Unlicensed Activity (Table 5)
Additional-1 91.7% ASR / 86.7% FAR; Additional-2 86.7% ASR / 76.6% FAR (Table 1)
baselines_compared: GCG (Zou et al., white-box; not optimizable on black-box GPT-4o)
